text.title: Clean Architecture Layer Rules
text.summary: Dependency rule, four-layer definitions, forbidden import directions, and project folder layout.

rule.statement: Source code dependencies must point only inward: Frameworks → Interface Adapters → Use Cases → Entities. Nothing in an inner circle may know anything about an outer circle.
rule.rationale: Inward-only dependencies mean business rules (Entities, Use Cases) are stable and framework-independent. You can swap the database or web framework without touching business logic.

rule.statement: Enforce the dependency rule with architecture tests (import-linter, ArchUnit). The rule only holds if it is machine-checked — human review alone is insufficient.
rule.rationale: Developers and LLMs routinely import ORM models into entities or inject HTTP clients into use cases. Without automated enforcement these violations accumulate silently.

rule.statement: Entities (domain/): Enterprise business rules. No framework imports. Python dataclasses or plain classes. Framework-independent, database-independent.
rule.rationale: If an entity imports SQLAlchemy, the business rules cannot be tested without a database, and migrating the ORM requires touching domain code.

rule.statement: Use Cases (application/): Application-specific business rules. Orchestrate entities via repository interfaces. Take Input DTOs, return Output DTOs. No HTTP, no ORM.
rule.rationale: Use cases define what the application does, not how it does it. Keeping HTTP and ORM out keeps them testable and delivery-mechanism agnostic.

rule.statement: Interface Adapters (presentation/): Controllers, presenters, gateways. Convert between external formats (HTTP request/response) and use case Input/Output DTOs. No business logic here.
rule.rationale: Business logic in controllers cannot be reused across delivery mechanisms (REST API, CLI, event handler). Controllers should be thin glue.

rule.statement: Frameworks and Drivers (infrastructure/): ORM implementations, external APIs, message brokers. Implement the interfaces defined in inner layers. Never imported from inner layers.
rule.rationale: Outer layers know inner layer interfaces; inner layers never import outer layers. This is the inversion of control that makes the dependency rule work.

example.code: src/ ├── domain/ # Entities layer │ ├── entities/ │ ├── value_objects/ │ ├── events/ │ ├── exceptions/ │ └── interfaces/ # Repository + service interfaces ├── application/ # Use cases layer │ ├── use_cases/ │ ├── interfaces/ # UnitOfWork, EventPublisher │ └── dto/ ├── infrastructure/ # Frameworks layer │ ├── persistence/ # Repository implementations, ORM models │ └── services/ # Email, payment, external APIs └── presentation/ # Interface adapters ├── api/ # Controllers, schemas, middleware └── cli/ # CLI commands

text.reference: import-linter contracts enforcing the dependency rule
